does: This paper investigates whether representations learned during text-to-image diffusion model pretraining can be repurposed for reward modeling without full retraining. The authors introduce DiT-Reward, which adapts a pretrained Diffusion Transformer by processing near-clean image latents and aggregating text-conditioned representations across transformer layers to predict human preferences. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple preference benchmarks including 85.6% accuracy on HPDv2 and 77.6% on HPDv3, surpassing the previous HPSv3 baseline when trained on identical data. The authors demonstrate that frozen backbones with lightweight learned heads can still extract meaningful preference signals, identify that middle-to-late layers contain strongest reward performance, and show consistent improvements with increased generative backbone capacity.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of generative modeling and reward learning for vision-language tasks. It extends prior research on text-to-image reward models like HPSv2/HPSv3, which trained reward predictors from scratch on preference data. The paper connects to broader work on representation learning and transfer learning in diffusion models, leveraging advances in Diffusion Transformers as generative backbones. It also relates to emerging paradigms around using generative model internals for downstream prediction tasks, and responds to questions about whether the rich hierarchical representations learned during large-scale image generation contain sufficient preference signal without additional supervised training.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more efficient reward model development by leveraging pretrained generative models rather than training from scratch, potentially reducing computational costs for preference learning. The findings enable future work on better understanding what representations diffusion models learn across their layers and how generative capacity correlates with evaluation capabilities. The work points toward broader applications of using frozen generative backbones with lightweight heads for other downstream tasks in vision-language understanding. When integrated with optimization methods like Flow-GRPO for text-to-image synthesis, it could improve the quality and human alignment of generated images at scale, potentially reducing the need for expensive preference data collection by transferring generative pretraining to evaluation.
